steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 2 concedes that studies report positive results but then pivots: methodological shortcomings — illustrated by Bergman's 19-student sample — make it impossible to draw firm conclusions. The correct answer must capture this critique of study design, not the content of the results themselves.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Although the programs may seem to produce positive results, close analysis of studies of the programs shows that they produce negative results just as frequently.": Text 2 says studies have methodological shortcomings, not that the programs produce negative results just as frequently as positive ones. ✗
  • "It is unknown what results the programs produce because studies of them are often designed in ways that do not allow for definitive conclusions.": This directly reflects Text 2's argument that small, flawed samples prevent any firm verdict on program effectiveness. ✓
  • "Most of the programs do not actually produce positive results even though program participants tend to regard them as beneficial.": Text 2 questions whether existing studies can establish positive effects; it does not conclude that most programs fail to produce positive results. ✗
  • "The programs tend to produce inconsistent results because they vary substantially in their methods and aims.": Text 2 criticizes the design of studies evaluating programs, not variation in the programs' own methods and aims. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
